name: Applications of Derivatives — Slopes, Maxima and Minima
whatIsIt: The derivative f′(x) is the slope of the curve. That single idea does a lot: where f′(x) > 0 the function is INCREASING, where f′(x) < 0 it is DECREASING, and where f′(x) = 0 the curve is momentarily flat — a possible MAXIMUM or MINIMUM (a turning point). The slope of the TANGENT line at a point is just f′ at that point.
whyNeeded: This is optimisation — finding the biggest profit, the least cost, the fastest route, the strongest box from a sheet of card. Engineers, economists and scientists set the derivative to zero to find best and worst cases.
realLifeUses: The largest volume box from a fixed sheet of card
Finding the highest point of a thrown object's path

vocabulary.meaning: Rising where f′(x) > 0, falling where f′(x) < 0.
Where the curve is momentarily flat: f′(x) = 0.
The slope of the line just touching the curve — equal to f′ at that point.
Finding the best (max or min) value by setting the derivative to zero.

story.title: The Highest Throw
story.text: Neel throws a ball; its height rises then falls. 'When is it highest?' asks Coach. 'When it stops rising and hasn't started falling — the slope is zero!' Neel realises. For the bowl-shaped curve y = x², the slope is f′ = 2x, which is 0 at x = 0 — its lowest point. 'Set the derivative to zero to find the turning point,' Coach nods.
story.extractedProblem: For y = x² (slope 2x), where is the turning point?
story.answerInStory: At x = 0 — because 2x = 0 gives x = 0, the minimum of the bowl.

shapes.label: tangent slope = f′(x); flat (slope 0) at a turning point

visual.caption: The tangent's slope is f′(x). It is positive going up, negative going down, and exactly zero at a maximum or minimum.

teachingGallery.title: Read the slope
teachingGallery.note: f′ > 0 rising, f′ < 0 falling, f′ = 0 flat (turning point).

examples.caption: Positive slope: the function is increasing.

shapes.label: tangent to y=x² at x=3 has slope 6
f′ = 0 at the bottom of the bowl

examples.caption: At the minimum the tangent is flat: f′ = 0.

standardMethod.summary: Differentiate to get f′(x). f′ > 0 → increasing, f′ < 0 → decreasing. Set f′(x) = 0 to find turning points. The tangent slope at a point is f′ there.
steps: Where f′(x) > 0 the curve rises; where f′(x) < 0 it falls.
Set f′(x) = 0 and solve to find turning points (max or min).
The slope of the tangent at x = a is f′(a).
Check whether a turning point is a max or min from the curve's shape.

workedExamples.problem: Find the slope of the tangent to y = x² at x = 3.
Where is the turning point of y = x²?

commonMistakes.mistake: Looking for a maximum or minimum where f′ is largest, instead of where f′ = 0.
commonMistakes.fix: At a turning point the curve is momentarily FLAT, so f′(x) = 0 — not where the slope is biggest.

commonMistakes.mistake: Mixing up increasing and decreasing signs of f′.
commonMistakes.fix: f′ > 0 means RISING (increasing); f′ < 0 means FALLING (decreasing).

easy.q: At a maximum or minimum, the derivative f′(x) equals?
Slope of the tangent to y = x² at x = 3 (f′ = 2x)?

medium.q: Slope of the tangent to y = x² at x = 4?
y = x² has its minimum at x = ? (solve 2x = 0)

challenge.q: For y = x², is the slope at x = 5 positive? Enter that slope (2x).

questions.q: Tangent slope to y = x² at x = 2?
Minimum of y = x² is at x = ?

teachBackPrompt: Draw y = x² for a grown-up and show the tangent slope f′ = 2x: it's 6 at x = 3 (rising) and 0 at x = 0 (the minimum). Explain that setting f′ = 0 finds the max/min.

revisionCard.summary: f′(x) is the slope. f′ > 0 increasing, f′ < 0 decreasing, f′ = 0 at a turning point (max/min). Tangent slope at x = a is f′(a). To optimise (biggest/smallest), set f′(x) = 0 and solve. For y = x²: slope 2x, minimum at x = 0.
